title: Smoke Signals

jokes.text: I fit my high school best friend into a suitcase way smaller than this when we were crossing the border between Laos and Vietnam, so I'm pretty sure I can figure this out.
jokes.setup: Someone offers Alexis help packing her overstuffed suitcase.
jokes.punchline: She casually references smuggling a person across an international border as evidence of her packing competence.
jokes.explanation: A standout Alexis joke — the casual delivery of an absurdly dark backstory (smuggling a human) as mundane problem-solving evidence is perfectly character-specific. Only Alexis has this history and only Alexis would deploy it so breezy. The economy is excellent: no wasted words, the premise escalates mid-sentence. Strong 8+ justified.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you so much for offering to help with my luggage. You know, I've actually moved around so much that I've become quite efficient at it. I once helped a man move seventeen suitcases across the Albanian border in the middle of the night. No questions asked. It was fine.

jokes.text: Yeah, you've mentioned that a couple times.
jokes.setup: Alexis enthusiastically talks about getting her beach curls back as though it's a novel aspiration.
jokes.punchline: David's weary, flat acknowledgment that she's been saying this repeatedly.
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose deadpan. The brevity is its strength — maximum tired-sibling energy in six words. Functional laugh, character-specific, but not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm so excited about these beach curls! They're going to be amazing!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, you've mentioned that. Multiple times. In the last five minutes.

jokes.text: Take it the 'Crows' movie is still shelved.
jokes.setup: Johnny says Moira has had 'a bit of a relapse' and can't leave the room.
jokes.punchline: David's instant, dry deduction — that Moira being in crisis means the film she's been pinning her comeback on is still dead — cuts through the euphemism.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical David line. He reads the subtext immediately and delivers a punchy deduction. Character-consistent and well-structured, with excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, she's been through a lot. Her career hasn't exactly been a straight line to success.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, we've started looking at wedding venues and no one chose to tell me?
jokes.setup: David and Patrick have been planning their wedding without including Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Alexis is offended at being left out despite having made clear she's leaving for six months and is mostly focused on herself.
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Alexis's obliviousness about her own absent-mindedness sets up the 'you invited Dad' riposte nicely. Serves as setup more than standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We wanted to keep it intimate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, there are like 200 people here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's intimate for a venue tour.

jokes.text: Touring a wedding venue is an incredibly intimate thing and we wanted to keep it small. — You invited Dad.
jokes.setup: David defends excluding Alexis by claiming the venue tour is an intimate affair they wanted to keep small.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately punctures this by pointing out Johnny (their father, not a member of the couple) was invited.
jokes.explanation: Tight two-line joke with excellent structure. David's excuse is self-evidently flimsy and Alexis's three-word riposte demolishes it perfectly. The economy of 'You invited Dad' as the entire punchline is outstanding. Very well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This latch and I have reached an impasse. It refuses to acknowledge my presence, and frankly, I find its behavior positively Shakespearean in its cruelty.

jokes.text: Okay, I don't know what that's supposed to mean, Alexis.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just devastatingly pointed out that David invited their father but not her.
jokes.punchline: David's flustered non-defense — he clearly knows exactly what it means and is embarrassed — is funnier than any comeback could be.
jokes.explanation: David's flustered retreat is character-true and extends the previous joke beat. The laugh is in his transparent denial. Works as a reaction beat extending joke #7.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, finally! I've been waiting for hours. Where have you been?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, it's David and Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I know who you are. Did you bring the croissants? I specifically asked for them to be warmed, not toasted.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, we're not room service.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you should be dressed for it. That tie is completely inadequate for hospitality.

jokes.text: Finally. I'll have the continental breakfast with the fresh-squeezed grapefruit juice. Orange Pekoe tea.
jokes.setup: Moira has been locked in a closet, apparently confused and disoriented. Johnny brings David and Patrick to see her.
jokes.punchline: Moira, still half-asleep and confused, mistakes her family for hotel staff and orders breakfast.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Moira. Even disoriented, she's imperious. The specific detail of 'Orange Pekoe tea' after the initial room service order lands the joke by grounding the absurdity in specificity. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I must respectfully decline your invitation to join this... peregrination. You see, I have a prior engagement that requires my immediate attention, and frankly, I haven't the fortitude to gallivant about the countryside like some common squire on holiday.

jokes.text: Sadly, I won't be able to squire you for today's wedding venue peregrination.
jokes.setup: Moira, freshly extracted from a closet after what sounds like a distressing night, needs to communicate that she can't join the venue tour.
jokes.punchline: She delivers this information in the most grandiloquent language possible — 'squire' and 'peregrination' — as though she is perfectly composed and hosting a formal occasion.
jokes.explanation: Moira's vocabulary is a running character joke throughout the series and this is a solid instance of it. 'Peregrination' for 'tour' is funny precisely because of the contrast with her disheveled situation. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She had a full meltdown. She's currently in the bedroom, lying on the bed, fully clothed, in the dark, listening to Celine Dion.

jokes.text: You invited Mom? She's literally been asleep in a closet for a week.
jokes.setup: David already complained about being excluded while Johnny invited their father. Now it's revealed Moira was also invited despite being completely incapacitated.
jokes.punchline: The escalating absurdity — they invited both parents, one of whom has been in a closet for a week — makes David's original exclusion even more cutting.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the 'invited everyone except Alexis' joke. 'Asleep in a closet for a week' is a funny specificity that reveals the absurdity of what's been happening offscreen. Works as callback and escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Six months? That's half a year! That's half a year of me not seeing you, and frankly, I don't think I have that kind of time left.

jokes.text: Does nobody care that I'm going to be gone for six months?
jokes.setup: Everyone is completely focused on their own crises and the wedding venue; Alexis is about to leave for six months.
jokes.punchline: Her plea is both genuine and ironic — she's the one who's been treating her departure as an escape, but now feels invisible.
jokes.explanation: Functional Alexis self-absorption joke. The irony of her wanting attention for the departure she's been celebrating is clear, but it's not especially sharp writing. Setup for later emotional beats.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is something on fire? I smell smoke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's my cologne.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it smells like a Victorian chimney sweep just expired in our living room.

jokes.text: Take me with you.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if nobody cares she'll be gone for six months. Johnny deflects; Moira is asked to respond.
jokes.punchline: Moira's simple, flat request — 'take me with you' — implies she wants to escape her own life just as much as Alexis does.
jokes.explanation: Three words carrying enormous comedic weight. Moira, having just emerged from a closet after what sounds like a breakdown, blurts out the most honest thing she's said — she wants out too. Perfect economy, perfectly Moira. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is it. This is how I go. Trapped in a walk-in closet like a discarded Valentino. David! Someone! Anyone!

jokes.text: See what you've done there? — Yeah.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just wrangled herself an invitation to join the venue tour by pointing out she needs a ride to the airport anyway.
jokes.punchline: Johnny and Patrick share a quiet, resigned acknowledgment that Alexis has outmaneuvered everyone in the room.
jokes.explanation: A nice reaction beat — the comedy is in the shared weary recognition. Brief, functional, adds texture without standing alone as a great joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm trapped! I'm going to be annulled in here!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This elevator is very chancy!

jokes.text: This is not how I go!
jokes.setup: Moira realizes she may be trapped in a smoke-filled closet.
jokes.punchline: Rather than screaming for help or trying to escape, her first instinct is to editorialize on the dramatic inadequacy of this death scenario.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. Utterly specific to Moira: she would reject a death that doesn't suit her narrative. The grammatical construction ('this is not HOW I go' — implying she has a preferred manner) is both funny and deeply character-revealing. Highly quotable, and earns its 8+ score comfortably — it's comparable in quality to 'Never go with a hippie to a second location' in economy and character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're fine. It's only 2 PM. I don't even start drinking until 4.

jokes.text: Oh, Stevie, thank God. I'm being annulled by a chancy door clasp!
jokes.setup: Stevie arrives to rescue Moira from the closet.
jokes.punchline: Moira uses 'annulled' (legally voided, typically a marriage term) when she means something like 'imperiled' or 'undone' — and blames 'a chancy door clasp' for her predicament.
jokes.explanation: Moira's malapropism here is inspired — 'annulled' is the wrong word used with such conviction it's funnier than any correct word would be. 'A chancy door clasp' as the agent of her near-demise is gloriously understated. Strong character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I mean, if he hits you, that's not okay. But if you hit him first, then technically he's defending himself, so that's fine.

jokes.text: Jeez, Moira, I wait 'til at least 10:30 before I have my first beer.
jokes.setup: Stevie has just extracted a disheveled, semi-conscious Moira from the closet.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's response to Moira's state isn't concern — it's a wry comment implying Moira must be drunk, delivered with perfect deadpan and a casual self-revelation about her own drinking habits.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Stevie line. The joke works on two levels: it's a comment on Moira's apparent state AND a self-deprecating reveal about Stevie's own relationship with morning drinking. The specific '10:30' makes it funnier than a round number. Very well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have to check on the babies. My girls need their evening moisture treatment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My wigs, David. Each one has been with me through different eras of my life. This is Simone — she was with me during my cabaret phase in Montreal. And this is Bernadette — she's been through two divorces and a stint in dinner theater.

jokes.text: Oh, my legs are in slumber. Carry me.
jokes.setup: Stevie has just freed Moira from the closet and is trying to get her moving.
jokes.punchline: Moira announces her legs are 'in slumber' (asleep) and commands Stevie to carry her — as though this is an entirely reasonable request.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'in slumber' instead of 'asleep' is vintage Moira. The flat imperative 'Carry me' — no question mark, no negotiation — is perfectly imperious. Short, punchy, and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: The venue looks nice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's very nice if you enjoy the aesthetic of a dead mall that's been repurposed into a wedding venue, which I do not.

jokes.text: You know, Moira, if Johnny locked you in that closet, we're going to have to call the police.
jokes.setup: Stevie is carrying/helping Moira out of a closet she was apparently trapped in all night.
jokes.punchline: Stevie deadpans a genuinely reasonable concern — that this might be domestic abuse — as though it's a perfectly casual thing to say while carrying someone.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry concern cuts through the chaos effectively. It's funny because it takes the absurd situation at face value and follows the logic to its natural conclusion. Good deadpan structure.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why didn't anyone tell me about my makeup?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, we didn't want to hurt your feelings. But now that you've asked, it looked like you were heading to an 80s themed funeral.

jokes.text: My babies... My girls. Lorna, second from the left. If she takes on smoke, she'll never recover. And Cindy! Cindy, below her. Cindy, I just gave her a blowout.
jokes.setup: Moira suddenly remembers something precious she left in the smoke-filled closet, crying out for her 'babies' and 'girls.'
jokes.punchline: The reveal that 'Lorna' and 'Cindy' are wigs — and Moira is more worried about them than any person — is a perfect Moira moment, capped by 'I just gave her a blowout.'
jokes.explanation: Outstanding bit. The misdirection (babies/girls sounds like children) and the slow reveal that these are named, beloved wigs is excellently structured. 'Cindy, I just gave her a blowout' as the final detail — treating the wig's wellbeing with maternal urgency — is both absurdist and completely character-true. Earns its 8+ rating: specific, surprising, quotable, and only possible with Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: She's been double-fisting margaritas all night.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Double-fisting? How is that even possible?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: One in each hand, obviously.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Right, but what about a third margarita?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You can't triple-fist, Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's the only venue for miles that doesn't look like a crime scene from a missing person docu-series.
jokes.setup: David and Patrick arrive at Elmbridge Manor for their venue tour.
jokes.punchline: David's backhanded compliment — the venue wins by not resembling a murder scene — is a perfectly cynical David take on rural options.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, specific David humor. The 'missing person docu-series' qualifier is a well-chosen specificity — funnier than just 'crime scene.' Good economy and strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, no.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're siblings.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He's my brother.

jokes.text: Uh, so no one thought to tell me I still have my show makeup on?
jokes.setup: The group arrives at the fancy venue. Stevie is there from the previous night's cabaret wrap party.
jokes.punchline: Stevie has arrived at an upscale wedding venue tour in full stage makeup, apparently unaware.
jokes.explanation: Functional character and visual joke — Stevie's obliviousness to her own appearance is consistent with her character. Sets up the venue manager's later comment nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: I apologize, I didn't realize you two were a couple.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: I mean, looking at her, I should have known she'd have to settle.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Excuse me?

jokes.text: I thought that was a choice.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks why no one told her she still has stage makeup on.
jokes.punchline: David casually implies the theatrical makeup looked intentional — a subtly brutal assessment of Stevie's normal aesthetic.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words that say everything. David's delivery implies stage makeup is indistinguishable from Stevie's usual look, which is both an insult and not quite one. Very efficient comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I think you're very talented and beautiful, and any man would be lucky to have you.

jokes.text: Yeah, I left Stevie double-fisting drinks in a hot tub at the cabaret wrap party last night. — I think I saw you triple-fisting at one point.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains why Stevie has stage makeup on — she left her partying hard the night before.
jokes.punchline: Stevie corrects the 'double-fisting' to 'triple-fisting,' escalating her own debauchery with complete nonchalance.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke. The one-upmanship is Stevie correcting Alexis in the wrong direction — not defending herself but doubling down. The casual delivery makes it funnier. Structure and economy are strong.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I found the perfect venue for the wedding.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's amazing! I can't believe you found somewhere without me. I mean, I would have found it faster, obviously, but still.

jokes.text: Oh no, not quite. — God, no.
jokes.setup: The venue manager assumes Alexis and Stevie (two women arriving together) are the happy couple getting married.
jokes.punchline: Both deny it simultaneously and with notable emphasis — Stevie's 'God, no' being particularly emphatic.
jokes.explanation: A reliable sitcom beat (mistaken identity at the venue) but Stevie's 'God, no' elevates it slightly. The contrast between the polite 'Oh no, not quite' and the blunt 'God, no' is the joke. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I was using the fire extinguisher, right? But the can said 'Inflammable.' So I thought, 'That means it'll make it worse!' So I used the opposite end.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Turns out 'inflammable' means the same thing as 'flammable.' Who knew?

jokes.text: Gentlemen, please forgive me. I looked at your friend's face and thought someone had just come from her makeup trial.
jokes.setup: The manager has just apologized for assuming the wrong couple. He then explains his mistake.
jokes.punchline: He explains he thought Stevie was in bridal makeup — a backhanded insult implying her stage makeup looks like wedding prep gone wrong.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed callback to the makeup joke — Clive's formal, polite delivery of an insult makes it funnier. The politeness of the framing while delivering a cutting comment is good situational comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I had always imagined my rescue would be more... mythological. You know, swept away on a white horse by some dashing hero. Not pulled out of a ditch by a man in a red nose and oversized shoes.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I'm glad I could help.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well. Beggars can't be choosers, can they?

jokes.text: Now, if our grooms and the... uh, other couple would like to follow me.
jokes.setup: Clive tries to address all four visitors — the engaged couple and their two companions — in one sentence.
jokes.punchline: His pause and uncertain 'uh, other couple' for Alexis and Stevie (who have just firmly denied being a couple) is both oblivious and vaguely insulting.
jokes.explanation: Extends the mistaken-couple bit. Clive's persistent (accidental?) misreading of Alexis and Stevie as a couple adds an extra beat of awkwardness. Functional running gag within the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm fixing the latches.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you see that smoke? That's a sign from the universe telling me I'm on the right path.

jokes.text: You look great.
jokes.setup: Stevie has just been told by the venue manager that he thought her makeup was a bridal trial. She looks mortified.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's flat, quiet 'You look great' is clearly not sincere — it's the most economical possible comfort that avoids the situation entirely.
jokes.explanation: Two words, perfectly deployed. Patrick's brand of kindness-as-comedy is a series hallmark — he says the nice thing while everyone knows it's untrue. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My career has been a tempestuous love affair. We've had our passionate highs, our devastating lows, and several restraining orders from various studios. But like any great romance, we keep finding our way back to each other, usually through my agent's persistence and complete disregard for my dignity.

jokes.text: David, I know you've made me relinquish most, if not all of the wedding planning to you at this point, but for what it's worth, this place is beautiful.
jokes.setup: Patrick and David tour the venue, which is clearly perfect.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's careful diplomatic framing — acknowledging David's total control before offering his mild opinion — reveals the power dynamic of their wedding planning with gentle comedy.
jokes.explanation: Gentle Patrick humor. The joke is in the excessive caveat before the simple compliment — he's walking on eggshells around David's wedding authority. Character-true and warm.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: If you need anything, I'm here for you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't need anything from anyone. I am a lone wolf.

jokes.text: See, David? Imagine if I wasn't here for this moment.
jokes.setup: David has just found 'the' venue. It's a big moment for him. Alexis is there because she was getting a ride to the airport.
jokes.punchline: Alexis retroactively claims credit for the moment as though her presence is essential, despite her being entirely incidental.
jokes.explanation: Classic Alexis self-insertion. She manages to make David's wedding venue discovery about her own presence. Character-consistent and mildly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've never felt more lucid.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm like a crystal-clear vessel. I'm like I did psilocybin with Pierre Trudeau and it actually worked.

jokes.text: Why do they call them toaster pops if it says on the box, 'Do not toast'?
jokes.setup: Roland has just started a fire by putting toaster pastries in a toaster.
jokes.punchline: Roland's aggrieved logic — the product name is misleading if the instructions forbid the implied use — is dumb but internally consistent, which is what makes it funny.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Roland joke. The comedic logic is airtight — he's right in a stupid way, and his genuine indignation makes it funnier. This is the kind of joke where the character's confidence in their own dumb reasoning IS the punchline. High character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Why don't we go for a little skincare swim? Au naturel. It's very vetements.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Not a shake of a lamb's rump between us.

jokes.text: How mercurial is life? We all imagine being carried from the ashes by the Goddess Artemis and here I get a balatron from Barnum and Bailey.
jokes.setup: Moira has just been rescued from a smoke-filled closet. Roland is explaining he caused it with toaster pops. Moira reflects on the experience.
jokes.punchline: Moira had apparently imagined a mythological rescue ('carried from the ashes by the Goddess Artemis') and instead got Roland ('a balatron from Barnum and Bailey' — a clown/buffoon).
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. Moira's grandiose expectations (Goddess Artemis) versus reality (Roland the buffoon) is a perfect encapsulation of her character. 'Balatron from Barnum and Bailey' is a brilliantly constructed insult — obscure enough to fit Moira's vocabulary, specific enough to be devastating, and the Barnum and Bailey detail is perfectly demeaning. Earns its 8+ rating.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, hello, this is Moira Rose calling. I'd like to inform you that I am, effective immediately, tech-nee-kah-lee unavailable.

jokes.text: I, I have no idea what that means, but it doesn't sound good. Okay, I'm gonna go to lunch. Gotta get this burnt toaster pop taste out of my mouth.
jokes.setup: Moira has just called Roland a 'balatron from Barnum and Bailey' with full dramatic conviction.
jokes.punchline: Roland's response is a two-part joke: first, his cheerful admission that he doesn't know what it means but senses it's bad; second, his pivot to getting lunch to address the burnt pop taste — treating this as a minor inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Roland's blitheness in the face of Moira's dramatic insult is great character comedy. His pivot to the practical concern of the toaster pop taste — while presumably still smelling of smoke — is absurdly grounded and very Roland.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are these prices in Canadian dollars or did someone just rob a bank?

jokes.text: I have been gifted a smoke signal and you will never again find me back in the closet.
jokes.setup: Johnny says he'll fix the latches. Moira says no need.
jokes.punchline: Moira frames being trapped in a smoky closet as a spiritual omen — a 'smoke signal' — that is telling her to retire from showbiz. The metaphor of 'out of the closet' (freedom) layered onto literal closet entrapment is either intentional or accidentally brilliant.
jokes.explanation: Elegant double meaning. 'Smoke signal' works literally (the fire that trapped her) and metaphorically (a sign from the universe). 'Never again find me back in the closet' plays on the idiom of coming out while describing a physical closet. The joke also serves as the episode's title callback. Strong writing that earns its 8+ rating through layered meaning and perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What about a tin package?

jokes.text: This may come as a shock to you, dear, but I don't believe my career has been that kind to me.
jokes.setup: Moira is declaring she wants to retire from showbiz.
jokes.punchline: The massive understatement — her career has been a catastrophic series of humiliations, not merely 'not kind' — delivered with theatrical gravity is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan understatement. The setup 'this may come as a shock' preceding what any observer would consider obvious is a classic ironic construction. Character-specific and well-delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Clive
dialogue.line: I'd be happy to help you with pricing. Though I have to say, it's quite brave of you to even attempt to sell anything. Most people in your... situation... would simply give things away.

jokes.text: It's time my career took its final bow.
jokes.setup: Moira is announcing her retirement.
jokes.punchline: The theatrical framing — a 'final bow' — for what is essentially quitting a job she hasn't had in years is characteristically grandiose Moira.
jokes.explanation: Functional Moira vocabulary joke — turning retirement into theatrical nomenclature. Character-consistent but not as sharp as her best lines in this episode.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: One month isn't enough time to plan a wedding.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We planned an intervention for my sister in a weekend.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And frankly, she was a worse case than most guests we'll have.

jokes.text: Oh, I have never needed less.
jokes.setup: Johnny says he'll be there if Moira needs anything.
jokes.punchline: Moira's declaration that she 'has never needed less' — a grammatically unusual construction meaning she needs nothing — is oddly beautiful and funny.
jokes.explanation: The construction 'I have never needed less' is a classically Moira formulation — grammatically unusual but semantically clear. The absolute confidence and brevity make it funny. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A short timeline will weed out the dead weight.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, are you calling me dead weight?

jokes.text: And I am including that Peruvian ayahuasca retreat we embarked upon with Al and Tipper.
jokes.setup: Moira is describing experiences where she has felt lucid or alive.
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning doing ayahuasca with Al and Tipper Gore as one of the Rose family's past experiences is an absurd celebrity name-drop that reveals the former life they led.
jokes.explanation: The celebrity drop earns its keep here because 'Al and Tipper' (Gore) is so specific and unexpected. The Gores are not obvious celebrity friends, which makes it funnier. The ayahuasca detail adds another layer of absurdity. Good specificity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was rejected by a very prestigious country club when I was eight years old. It was devastating. And I have spent the better part of my adult life trying to secure membership to another one. So when I say that this wedding is important to me, I mean it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's insane.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, what's insane is your trip to Bali, but we don't talk about that.

jokes.text: Smiles over here please. Ted wants to know what we're doing. Okay, like, a little less eager and maybe one without Stevie. No offense.
jokes.setup: The group is touring the venue and Alexis is taking photos to send to Ted.
jokes.punchline: Alexis directing the group photo, including casually requesting Stevie be excluded — 'no offense' — while remaining totally unaware of how rude this is.
jokes.explanation: Solid Alexis obliviousness. The 'no offense' after asking Stevie to leave the frame is a nice cringe beat. Character-true and functional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You look like you're wearing a tankini.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Excuse me? A tankini? That is NOT what this is.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a one-piece swimsuit with a tank top.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know what a tankini is, David. I'm offended that you would suggest I would ever wear something so... practical.

jokes.text: Are these in dollars?
jokes.setup: Clive presents the pricing guide. David reviews it.
jokes.punchline: David's horrified first question — not about packages or services but whether the currency is correct — implies the prices are so high he's hoping for a misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: Economy is exceptional — three words convey shock, class anxiety, and desperate hope. A great sitcom beat that works on multiple levels. The Rose family's financial fall makes this particularly pointed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I want to apologize. I'm sorry that you felt the way you felt about the thing that happened, which I may or may not have done, depending on your interpretation of the events in question.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, that's not really an apology.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, let me finish. I'm also sorry that my apology wasn't what you were looking for, and I'm further sorry that you have these expectations of apologies that don't align with my particular communication style, which is really more about clarity and nuance than emotional availability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? I'm being very sincere. Furthermore, I regret that this entire situation has transpired in a manner that has caused you discomfort, though I maintain that many of the facts you've presented are subjective at best.

jokes.text: Is there a package lower than the bronze package? — Perhaps a copper package? — No, we can't accommodate that.
jokes.setup: The pricing guide is way beyond the Roses' budget. David asks for something cheaper.
jokes.punchline: Alexis helpfully suggests 'a copper package' (logically the next metal down from bronze); Clive firmly shuts this down.
jokes.explanation: Strong three-beat joke. Alexis's 'copper package' is a genuinely clever escalation of the metallic tier system, and Clive's curt refusal is perfect timing. The joke is well-structured and each participant plays their role correctly.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Why are you wearing heels on a plane?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because I'm not going to dress down just because I'm in economy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Economy?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, economy class. I've never been in it before, but I assume it's like business class, just... more economical.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Alexis, economy class doesn't have the same amenities as business class.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What do you mean? Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Like... leg room, better food, lie-flat seats...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. So what you're saying is I'm going to be sitting in a tiny chair for six hours in heels?

jokes.text: Did you not ask to see the prices in advance? — They don't provide... — We don't provide pricing in advance. — Well, now we know why.
jokes.setup: The prices have come as a shock. Alexis asks why no one checked in advance.
jokes.punchline: David's withering 'Well, now we know why' — directed at Clive's policy of not sharing prices upfront — implies the venue knows it needs to lure people in before revealing the cost.
jokes.explanation: David's comeback is sharp and cuts right to the inference. The momentary confusion between Patrick and Clive over who is speaking adds a nice awkward beat before David lands it. Well-structured multi-person joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was lying there thinking, if I don't make it through this, Johnny's going to be absolutely fine. He'll probably buy a condo in Florida, take up golf, maybe finally get that boat he's always wanted.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: He'll meet some woman named Brenda at a shuffleboard tournament, and they'll be very happy together. She'll probably decorate better than I ever could.

jokes.text: This might be the worst day of my life.
jokes.setup: David is reviewing unaffordable pricing at his dream wedding venue.
jokes.punchline: The hyperbole — this pricing guide being 'the worst day of his life' — is funny from a character who has experienced genuine catastrophe (losing everything, exile in a small town).
jokes.explanation: Functional David hyperbole. The joke is in the disproportionate distress. Not as sharp as some of his other lines in this episode.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I lost a very expensive watch that night, but it was worth every penny.

jokes.text: Okay, look. You're clearly very desperate and I'm feeling terribly for you. — I don't love the way you phrase that.
jokes.setup: Clive is about to offer a deal on the venue because David is clearly stressed.
jokes.punchline: David's quiet, dignified objection to being called 'very desperate' — while being very desperate — is a perfect understated response.
jokes.explanation: Nice cringe beat — David's dignity is wounded even as he's about to accept charity from a wedding venue manager. The mild protest while accepting the premise is very David.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We should seize the moment. Life is short. Any one of us could die tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Hit by a bus. Struck by lightning. Aneurysm while tying your shoe.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Crushed by a falling air conditioner unit while standing on a street corner in Manhattan in 1987.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So really, we should be living our best lives right now.

jokes.text: This is basically free!
jokes.setup: Clive has offered a 30% discount off an already-discounted date for a high-end wedding venue.
jokes.punchline: David's characterization of a still-expensive venue as 'basically free' because of the discount reveals both his excitement and his warped relationship with money.
jokes.explanation: Good David character note — his former wealthy mindset reactivated by any discount. 'Basically free' is a funny absolutism. Short and punchy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, okay? I'm just... I'm tired of pretending that I have it all figured out. I don't. And yeah, maybe I drink too much, but at least when I'm drinking, I don't have to think about how much I don't know what I'm doing.

jokes.text: Uh, I planned my ex girlfriend's intervention in less than an hour. Plus, it'll weed out all the dead weight.
jokes.setup: Patrick says a month isn't enough notice for a wedding.
jokes.punchline: David's rebuttal is a dark reveal (he planned an intervention for an ex in under an hour) followed by a callous dismissal of unwanted guests as 'dead weight.'
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke that works well. The ex-girlfriend intervention detail is a perfect dark reveal of David's past. 'Weed out the dead weight' as a wedding planning philosophy is sharp and character-specific. Well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What do you mean you can't check in? Your flight is in like an hour.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but the website won't let me. It says my reservation doesn't exist.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Did you try using your confirmation number?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Of course I did. I'm not an idiot, Stevie. Well, not about flying anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Let me see your confirmation email.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, here it is.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Alexis, your flight is in December.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, I know that.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Of next year.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No! It says 12/15. December 15th!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Alexis, that's the international date format. Month slash day. It's the 15th of December.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Um... I will not be here in one month, so I guess that makes me the dead weight?
jokes.setup: David says a quick wedding date will 'weed out all the dead weight.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis slowly realizes she is the 'dead weight' David is referring to — and her hurt, quiet realization is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat and callback. The slow dawning of Alexis's realization is funnier than an immediate response would be. Character-true and serves the emotional arc as well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need you to leverage my core competencies and synergize my market position. We need to create a paradigm shift in my brand visibility. This isn't happenstance, darling—this is Kismetic.

jokes.text: I'm feeling a little tension.
jokes.setup: There is clearly enormous sibling tension in the room after David's 'dead weight' comment.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's massive understatement — quietly noting he 'feels a little tension' in a room that has just exploded — is perfectly Patrick.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's role as the even-keeled diplomat is well-captured in five words. The gap between the obvious tension and his mild observation is the comedy. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: Your husband was found naked in the creek.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: He said he was having a 'holistic wellness experience' and that this town doesn't appreciate the finer things.

jokes.text: Why? She's the one that can't wait to get out of here. I once dated someone who left me for a stuffed animal. I'm not having my big day... Our... Our big day compromised because Alexis wanted to chill in a tankini for six months.
jokes.setup: Patrick says they can't get married without Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David's rant escalates through: (1) the tankini jab, (2) a completely random and dark reveal that he once dated someone who left him for a stuffed animal, (3) self-correcting 'my big day' to 'our big day,' and (4) returning to 'tankini' as the culminating grievance.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding David rant. The stuffed animal detail is completely left-field and therefore hilarious — it's a dark reveal dropped in mid-sentence and never explained. The self-correction from 'my big day' to 'our big day' is a perfect character beat (David's unconscious ownership of the wedding). 'Tankini' as both a specific fashion crime and the culminating insult is great. This rant earns its 8+ easily.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How much are we talking? Because I have a Hermès scarf collection that I'm willing to part with, but I draw the line at the vintage Pucci.

jokes.text: Honestly, David, if this is the kind of bride you're going to be, I'm glad that I'm leaving.
jokes.setup: David has just delivered a rant ending with 'tankini.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis calling David a 'bride' — not a groom — is a pointed dig at his controlling behavior over wedding planning.
jokes.explanation: Sharp Alexis comeback. 'Bride' is the exact right word — it captures David's controlling aesthetic ownership of the wedding while serving as a pointed insult. Well-targeted.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I assure you, Johnny will remain fully habilimented.

jokes.text: And I would never wear a tankini!
jokes.setup: Alexis calls David a 'bride' and storms off. David presumably runs after her.
jokes.punchline: David's final shout is not a defense of being called a bride or a resolution of the conflict — it's an indignant denial of the tankini slur, revealing his true priority.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best punchline. What makes it exceptional: David ignores the 'bride' accusation entirely and fixates exclusively on the fashion slander of 'tankini.' The hierarchy of his concerns is laid bare. The callback to the tankini insult planted earlier in the same rant pays off perfectly. This is elite sitcom writing — specific, character-revealing, and completely surprising in what it prioritizes. Genuinely earns a 9+ on impact.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was stuck in the creek, completely submerged. I couldn't get out. I was thrashing around, panicking, and then the police arrived with their fishing line and had to reel me in like some sort of... large mouth bass.

jokes.text: Well, I guess congratulations are in order. — We didn't end up taking the venue. — Really? What stopped you? Did they like, run out of sandwiches or...?
jokes.setup: Patrick tells Stevie they didn't take the venue after all the excitement.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'Did they run out of sandwiches?' is a perfectly dry, absurdist guess at what could have derailed things — implying the Roses are so high-maintenance that a sandwich shortage is plausible.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Stevie deadpan. The 'sandwiches' guess is both absurd and specific enough to feel plausible for this family. The trailing 'or...' invites the audience to imagine other ridiculous possibilities. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they said they loved my performance, but there was a note about the lighting in the final scene. Apparently, I was upstaging Johnny's... birthday tuxedo.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, I was not wearing a tuxedo.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's what I told them.

jokes.text: Uh, yes. But that's not why.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks 'Did they run out of sandwiches?' expecting it to be a joke.
jokes.punchline: Patrick confirms that yes, there was in fact a sandwich situation, but that wasn't the reason — validating Stevie's absurd guess while maintaining the actual story.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect subversion. The joke hinges on the impossible expectation that Stevie's absurd guess was actually correct. 'Uh, yes. But that's not why.' confirms the premise while withholding the laugh — and the revelation that they did take bacon from the venue (referenced later) makes it retrospectively even better. Earns its 8 rating through superb economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The car situation is actually more complicated than you'd think.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Clive won't take it to a mechanic because he has a phobia of being in enclosed spaces with people he doesn't know.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So we asked if he'd let us take it, but he won't let the car leave the property because he has a phobia of separation anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And we can't fix it here because apparently he also has a phobia of loud noises, bright lights, and people named Derek.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The only mechanic available is named Derek.

jokes.text: David apologizing: 'I might have overreacted and for that, I am apologetic.' — 'So, you're sorry.' — 'I knew there was a reason I came on this trip. My God. I am remorseful over an action I participated in.'
jokes.setup: Patrick pushes David to apologize to Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David's apology is delivered in increasingly convoluted, distancing language — 'apologetic,' then 'remorseful over an action I participated in' — as though grammatical distance from the apology makes it less painful to give.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best extended jokes. The escalating abstraction of David's apology language — from 'apologetic' to 'remorseful over an action I participated in' — is a masterclass in character comedy. The passive construction 'an action I participated in' (rather than 'something I did') is the peak of his avoidance. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific. Earns its 8+ comfortably.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, I actually really appreciate you. Like, genuinely.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well. That's... I appreciate that. Thank you for saying that.

jokes.text: Car won't start. — What?! Well, I can't be late for my flight!
jokes.setup: The group is leaving the venue. The car won't start.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's immediate pivot to her own inconvenience — 'I can't be late for my flight!' — after Alexis just had a heartfelt moment shows her self-absorption reasserting itself instantly.
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat. Alexis's self-absorption re-emerging after a genuine moment is character-true. Not especially sharp writing but serves the plot.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You don't even know what day it is.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I do know what day it is. I went to Billy Zane's wedding, and I didn't know what day that was either.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You did not go to Billy Zane's wedding.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I went to a wedding. It might have been his.

jokes.text: Why do I feel like Clive doesn't handle stress well?
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests they go inside to ask Clive for help with the car.
jokes.punchline: David has read Clive correctly — his instinctive assessment of the venue manager's fragility suggests he's been watching him carefully.
jokes.explanation: Good observational David line. Foreshadowing Clive's reveal (he doesn't drive because he's scared of car horns) makes this a proto-callback. David's social reading of people is character-specific and this is a nice deployment of it.

dialogue.speaker: Clive
dialogue.line: Well, it's Tuesday. We process the pigs on Tuesday.

jokes.text: This might be a stupid question, but why are you wearing high heels on an airplane? — Oh, I'm not wearing them on the plane, they hand out slippers with the mimosas. — When was the last time you flew economy?
jokes.setup: Stevie notices Alexis is wearing high heels, apparently for a long flight.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's explanation — that she'll swap them for the complimentary slippers — reveals she doesn't know economy passengers don't get slippers and mimosas. Stevie's follow-up 'When was the last time you flew economy?' lands the kicker.
jokes.explanation: Excellent fish-out-of-water joke that lands on multiple levels: Alexis's former life (first class always), her current circumstances (can't afford it), and her total unawareness of the gap. Stevie's kicker is perfectly calibrated. One of the episode's stronger laugh moments. Strong structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It sounds like a Céline Dion ballad being murdered.

jokes.text: Hard to believe this is our first trip down to the creek.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira are taking a romantic walk to the creek for the first time despite living there for years.
jokes.punchline: The mild absurdity of having lived in a small town near a creek for so long without visiting it — because the Roses would never have considered exploring their environment — is the gentle joke.
jokes.explanation: A gentle observational note rather than a proper joke. The comedy is implicit in the Roses' avoidance of anything rustic. Low impact on its own but sets a warm tone.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, when life gives you a venue disaster, you gotta make... venue-monade.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've been saying that for years.

jokes.text: To think I nearly made you a widower. Oh, John, I can just see you aimlessly wandering through life. Stoic but stymied, not a friend in the world and so very lost.
jokes.setup: Moira reflects on her near-death experience in the closet.
jokes.punchline: Rather than mourning what Johnny would have lost, Moira describes his imagined widowhood as a pathetic picture of a man utterly dependent on her — with no friends and no direction.
jokes.explanation: Moira's self-centeredness made romantic — she frames Johnny's imagined grief as proof of his inadequacy without her. 'Stoic but stymied' is a lovely phrase. The joke is in how completely self-congratulatory her sympathy for him is.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've said that before.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, you haven't.

jokes.text: Does this not remind you of that wellness retreat we went to in Evian, right after Alexis ended things with Sean Penn?
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira are sitting by the creek, which is a modest rural creek.
jokes.punchline: Johnny fondly compares this to a luxury wellness retreat in Evian, and casually drops that their daughter once dated Sean Penn.
jokes.explanation: The Evian comparison to a Canadian creek is gently funny, but the real joke is the incidental 'after Alexis ended things with Sean Penn' — a completely casual celebrity revelation that tells us a great deal about the former Rose family life. Well-integrated character detail.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's fine, the trip got delayed. It's actually perfect because now David and I can go together, and I get to finally see where Ted lives.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Apparently his house requires you to remove your shoes at the door, which is... a lot.

jokes.text: Bid adieu to our clothing and let the cool sting of the mineral water remind us that we were, in fact, alive and well. — I lost my Patek Philippe in that lake, but I wouldn't have had it any other way.
jokes.setup: Moira recalls their romantic skinny-dipping at the Evian retreat.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's contribution to this romantic memory is that he lost a very expensive watch in the lake — but his rueful acceptance of this loss as worth it is perfectly Johnny.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's contribution to the romantic story being the loss of a luxury watch (Patek Philippe — extremely expensive) is a perfectly calibrated touch. His philosophical acceptance of the loss ('but I wouldn't have had it any other way') is gentle and warm comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your father got arrested at the farmers market.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? Why would you lead with that so casually?

jokes.text: Moira, that was 15 years ago! — What if someone sees us?
jokes.setup: Moira is suggesting they reenact their skinny-dipping memory at the local creek.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flustered anxiety — 'What if someone sees us?' — is both practical and funny in the context of a man who apparently had no problem with this 15 years ago.
jokes.explanation: Functional character reversal — Moira is the bold one, Johnny the timid one, which is slightly against type in an amusing way. Sets up the indecent exposure payoff.

jokes.text: You could be hit by a Mack truck, or bopped on the head by a tiny piece of space debris!
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to convince Johnny to seize the moment at the creek.
jokes.punchline: Her carpe diem argument escalates from a mundane danger (truck) to a gloriously specific and unlikely one ('bopped on the head by a tiny piece of space debris').
jokes.explanation: The contrast between 'Mack truck' and 'tiny piece of space debris' is excellent — the second option is so specific, so Moira, and so improbable. 'Bopped' is the perfect verb. The escalation from mundane to cosmic danger in one breath is strong Moira writing.

jokes.text: You just remove those vetements, Mr. Rose, I'll be back in the shake of a lamb's rump.
jokes.setup: Moira is about to skinny dip with Johnny but first needs to make a phone call.
jokes.punchline: She uses 'vetements' (French for clothes — also a fashion brand) and the archaic 'shake of a lamb's rump' (a variation on 'shake of a lamb's tail'), delivered with total imperious confidence.
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Moira vocabulary joke executed at peak level. 'Vetements' for clothes is either pretentious or genuinely French (or both — the Roses would use French casually). 'Shake of a lamb's rump' instead of the standard idiom is an unexpected specificity that makes it funnier. Highly quotable and earns its 8+ rating.

jokes.text: I'm moving to a place that doesn't have a Sephora for literally 2700 miles, so I'm sorry if I'm trying to convince myself that I'm doing the right thing.
jokes.setup: Stevie tells Alexis her constant enthusiasm about leaving might be hurting David's feelings.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's real reason for her performative excitement — she's terrified and compensating — is revealed, but delivered through the lens of Sephora proximity, which is both funny and Alexis-specific.
jokes.explanation: A surprisingly vulnerable moment delivered through comedy. The '2700 miles from Sephora' is simultaneously funny and genuinely revealing — Alexis measures her sacrifice in beauty retail terms. The specific mileage is a great detail. Strong character integration and earned emotional resonance.

jokes.text: I've actually been jealous of your trip. Like, you're getting out there, seeing the world.
jokes.setup: Stevie, who has just done a cabaret show (a major personal triumph), admits she's jealous of Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Stevie, who spent most of the series refusing to want more, admitting envy of someone leaving to assist on a scientific expedition — as 'getting out there, seeing the world' — is funny in its understatement of the Galapagos.
jokes.explanation: More emotional beat than joke, but there's warmth and gentle comedy in Stevie's admission. Sets up the compliment scene.

jokes.text: I just wish I had your... what's going on? Because I think I might be in the middle of giving you a compliment for the first time, so.
jokes.setup: Stevie is in the middle of complimenting Alexis when Alexis appears distracted.
jokes.punchline: Stevie self-narrates that she thinks she's giving a compliment 'for the first time' — a meta-acknowledgment that this is out of character for her — and seems almost confused by her own behavior.
jokes.explanation: Stevie commenting on her own uncharacteristic behavior mid-compliment is a meta-comic moment that works beautifully. The trailing 'so.' is perfect — she doesn't know how to finish the sentence. Strong character integration and warm comedy.

jokes.text: Okay. Um, think I've found the problem; your flight's not leaving for another month.
jokes.setup: Alexis is panicking about missing her flight; Stevie takes her phone to help.
jokes.punchline: Stevie discovers the problem — Alexis has had the date wrong all along.
jokes.explanation: Plot-functional joke setup — works primarily as setup for the date confusion reveal. Stevie's delivery ('think I've found the problem') is characteristically understated.

jokes.text: Yeah, but it's day before month, so you're not leaving on July 8th, you're leaving on August 7th.
jokes.setup: Alexis insists her ticket says 07-08 meaning July 8th.
jokes.punchline: Stevie explains the European date format — day/month, not month/day — revealing Alexis has been counting down to the wrong date.
jokes.explanation: Good comedic reveal — the date confusion is both plausible and character-specific to Alexis (she's been many places and never learned the format). The practical consequence (stranded for a month) gives the joke weight.

jokes.text: Maybe you could start by telling David that you messed up and he can book the venue after all!
jokes.setup: Alexis is despairing about what to do; her ticket doesn't allow changes.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's cheerful, very practical suggestion — go tell David, who benefits from this — is funny because Alexis's pride makes this the last thing she wants to do.
jokes.explanation: Functional but pointed. Stevie cuts through the drama with the obvious solution. The comedy is in Alexis's response — she'd rather sleep at the airport.

jokes.text: Okay, I would literally rather sleep at the airport for a month.
jokes.setup: Stevie suggests Alexis tell David she was wrong.
jokes.punchline: Alexis would genuinely rather live in an airport for thirty days than admit a mistake to David.
jokes.explanation: Good character note — the Rose sibling dynamic is perfectly captured in Alexis choosing airport homelessness over an apology. The hyperbole works because it's plausible for Alexis.

jokes.text: Oh, what an emphatically Kismetic turn of events. Oh, John's here now. Okay, talk soon. Kisses!
jokes.setup: Moira has just called her agent to retire, and immediately receives news that the Crows movie has been picked up.
jokes.punchline: Moira's reaction to the news is delivered as a performance for her agent — 'Kismetic' (her version of 'karmic' or 'kismet') — and the breezy 'Kisses!' send-off undercuts the drama of the moment.
jokes.explanation: Moira's invented word 'Kismetic' (presumably merging 'kismet' and... something) and the airy 'Kisses!' are both great character touches. The comedy is entirely in the vocabulary and delivery.

jokes.text: Hello ma'am, I'm Officer Cornwall. I was off duty when I came across this man in a state of undress, down by the creek. He claims to be your husband?
jokes.setup: Moira has been on the phone making a triumphant call to her agent while Johnny waited naked at the creek.
jokes.punchline: The payoff of Johnny's nakedness: he's been found by an off-duty cop who's dragged him back to confront Moira.
jokes.explanation: Well-structured comedic payoff to the skinny-dipping setup. The officer's formal language ('state of undress') and his qualifying 'He claims to be your husband' (implying even he's not sure) are nicely handled.

jokes.text: Last I checked, a decades-long successful marriage is not a criminal offence.
jokes.setup: The officer has confirmed he found Moira's husband naked at the creek.
jokes.punchline: Moira responds as though the officer is accusing her of the marriage, rather than addressing Johnny's nudity.
jokes.explanation: Moira's deflection is perfectly executed — she changes the subject to the marriage itself and positions herself as the one being questioned. The word 'decades-long' is also gently funny as a flex. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I found your husband sunning himself in the creek, like he was in some Grecian bath.
jokes.setup: The officer explains what Johnny was doing.
jokes.punchline: 'Sunning himself in the creek, like he was in some Grecian bath' is an unexpectedly poetic and specific description from a small-town cop.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Grecian bath' from a rural officer is nicely unexpected. The image is funny and the officer's description is richer than his function in the scene requires.

jokes.text: No, I don't think we need to editorialize.
jokes.setup: The officer has described Johnny as 'sunning himself like he was in a Grecian bath.'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's mild objection to the colorful description — 'I don't think we need to editorialize' — is perfectly dignified for a man currently in a state of undress.
jokes.explanation: Johnny maintaining his corporate dignity while presumably disheveled and embarrassed is classic Johnny. 'Editorialize' is the perfect word choice — formal, authoritative, completely at odds with his situation. Good economy.

jokes.text: Officer, perhaps we could arrange a plea bargain?
jokes.setup: The officer has confirmed Johnny committed indecent exposure.
jokes.punchline: Moira suggesting a 'plea bargain' to a cop who showed up on her doorstep — as though she's in a courtroom drama — is both legally absurd and perfectly theatrical.
jokes.explanation: Moira treating a roadside conversation with a cop like a legal proceeding is good character comedy. The word 'arrange' is very Moira — formal and transactional.

jokes.text: Well, since I wasn't supposed to be fly fishing at this end of the creek... I think we can leave it at a warning.
jokes.setup: Moira asks if they can arrange a plea bargain.
jokes.punchline: The officer reveals he also wasn't supposed to be there — he was poaching fishing spots — so both parties have something to hide. Mutual compromise achieved.
jokes.explanation: A neat ironic twist — the officer had his own rule-breaking reason to be there, so mutual non-disclosure is the logical outcome. Well-structured and satisfying. Moira's 'plea bargain' request retroactively becomes accurate.

jokes.text: Thank you, Officer, for your almost militant commitment to the protection of our community. I promise to keep my husband habilimented from now on.
jokes.setup: The officer has agreed to let them off with a warning.
jokes.punchline: Moira's farewell is characteristically excessive — 'almost militant commitment' for a guy who let them off, and 'habilimented' (clothed) as a grandiose word for the simple act of keeping Johnny dressed.
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable Moira line. 'Habilimented' is an extremely rare word for clothed, and the commitment to using it in this context is peak Moira vocabulary comedy. The 'almost militant commitment' qualifier (not quite militant, but nearly) is a nice touch. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: What happened was, you left me alone, struggling to get out of a creek! The algae was so slippery, the cop had to throw me his fishing line.
jokes.setup: Moira starts excitedly sharing news. Johnny interrupts to describe what actually happened while she was on the phone.
jokes.punchline: The undignified reality of Johnny's situation — struggling in algae while a cop fishes him out with a literal fishing line — is a perfectly unglamorous image.
jokes.explanation: The specific image of an off-duty cop throwing Johnny a fishing line to get him out of a slippery creek is vivid and funny. The contrast with Moira's triumphant phone call happening simultaneously makes it better. Good physical comedy conveyed through dialogue.

jokes.text: Well, button back up your birthday tux, John, because I've just found out the 'Crows' movie is back in business!
jokes.setup: Johnny is aggrieved about being left naked in a creek. Moira ignores this entirely.
jokes.punchline: 'Birthday tux' as Moira's phrase for Johnny's naked body is a classic Moira euphemism — grandiose, theatrical, and slightly absurd.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Moira phrase. 'Birthday tux' for being naked is inspired — it takes the common idiom 'birthday suit' and elevates it with the word 'tux' (formal wear), which both makes it funnier and reveals Moira's inability to use ordinary language even for ordinary things. Highly quotable. Earns its 8+ rating for originality and character specificity.

jokes.text: At the precise moment I choose to walk away, the industry comes crawling back to me on all fours.
jokes.setup: Moira had just decided to retire and called her agent — only to immediately get career-reviving news.
jokes.punchline: Moira frames the coincidence as evidence of her cosmic importance — the industry didn't move on, it was waiting for her to look away so it could beg.
jokes.explanation: Moira's self-mythologizing at its finest — 'crawling back to me on all fours' is a great image. Her interpretation of a coincidence as confirmation of her indispensability is extremely in character.

jokes.text: It appears officer fly fish has taken his leave, how about that dip? — Oh, I think the moment has passed.
jokes.setup: After the indecent exposure incident, Moira suggests they continue with the skinny-dipping plan.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's extremely dry 'I think the moment has passed' after being found naked by a cop and rescued with a fishing line is perfectly understated.
jokes.explanation: A good button on the whole creek storyline. Johnny's understatement is perfect — 'the moment has passed' is doing enormous work. Good economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Okay, roadside assistance isn't gonna be here for the next hour, so we're going to call you a cab. — And Clive didn't have jumper cables because he doesn't drive because he's scared of car horns.
jokes.setup: Patrick is solving the car problem.
jokes.punchline: David delivers the reveal: Clive is scared of car horns, which is why he doesn't drive, which is why he has no jumper cables — a cascade of specificity that confirms David's earlier intuition.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and payoff to David's earlier hunch about Clive. 'Scared of car horns' is a perfectly specific and unexpected phobia for a wedding venue manager. The causal chain (scared of horns → doesn't drive → no jumper cables) is absurdly logical. Well-structured.

jokes.text: Like that time you showed up to Kate Winslet's wedding a month late. — The calligraphy was hard to read and Billy Zane also did the same thing, David.
jokes.setup: David mocks Alexis for mixing up her flight date, comparing it to a past incident.
jokes.punchline: Alexis defends herself by noting: (1) the calligraphy was hard to read, and (2) Billy Zane also made the same mistake — a celebrity accomplice to her error.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The revelation that Alexis showed up to Kate Winslet's actual wedding a month late is an absurd piece of Rose family history. But 'Billy Zane also did the same thing' is the real genius — it's a defense that only makes things worse while simultaneously being a celebrity cameo story. The calligraphy excuse is a perfect Alexis rationalization. Highly quotable and very rewatch-worthy.

jokes.text: Okay, well the upside is that you guys can now take the place, so actually, you're welcome.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just admitted she mixed up her dates and will be here for another month.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately pivots from humiliation to credit — 'you're welcome' for a mistake that accidentally solved their problem.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Alexis — the instantaneous reframe of her mistake as a gift to others. 'Actually, you're welcome' as the coda is a great character beat. Swift and punchy.

jokes.text: Do we go back and talk to Clive?! Ew.
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests they go back to book the venue with Clive.
jokes.punchline: David's 'Ew' — directed at the prospect of talking to Clive again — is a perfectly petulant reaction that reveals David's residual annoyance at the whole venue saga.
jokes.explanation: David's petulant 'Ew' as a complete thought is character-specific and economical. The comedy is in the complete disproportionality of his reaction. Short and effective.

jokes.text: Oh my God. — What is that? It's like a thousand babies teething.
jokes.setup: The group returns to the venue and is greeted by a horrible sound.
jokes.punchline: David's description — 'a thousand babies teething' — for the sound of the neighboring farm processing pork is vivid and dramatic.
jokes.explanation: David's simile is vividly unpleasant and funny. 'A thousand babies teething' is specific enough to land as an image. Good character voice.

jokes.text: Uh, that is correct. They process their pork on the first Sunday of every month, hence our discounted rates. Did I hear that the wedding was back on?
jokes.setup: The horrific sound is traced to the neighboring farm. David asks about it.
jokes.punchline: Clive casually explains the discount mystery (pork processing = terrible noise) and immediately pivots to business — 'Did I hear that the wedding was back on?' — as though this is all perfectly normal.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the 'discounted Sunday' mystery is excellent — the discount exists because of the horrendous farm noise. Clive's cheerful pivot back to booking ('Did I hear the wedding was back on?') while chaos is audible is a great beat. Well-structured revelation.

jokes.text: Hey. This is not a winner, Clive.
jokes.setup: Clive has just explained why the venue is discounted (farm noise) and asks if the wedding is back on.
jokes.punchline: David's flat, direct 'This is not a winner, Clive' is a devastatingly simple verdict.
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect. 'This is not a winner' is a perfectly David-esque verdict — corporate, final, slightly euphemistic. Addressing Clive by name adds a personal dimension. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I can't stop hearing it! Like a thousand guinea pigs were being shoved into a wood chipper.
jokes.setup: David has just said it's not a winner due to the farm noise.
jokes.punchline: He escalates from 'thousand babies teething' to 'thousand guinea pigs being shoved into a wood chipper' — a darker, more violent image that reveals how the noise is affecting him.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of his earlier simile. The progression from babies teething to guinea pigs in a wood chipper is darkly funny and shows David's increasing distress. The specificity of 'guinea pigs' rather than a more expected animal is the right choice. Quotable.

jokes.text: Well, it sounds to me like you two dodged a bacon-wrapped bullet. I'll say it again: life is but a strand of happy accidents.
jokes.setup: David has decided the venue is not viable. Roland comments.
jokes.punchline: 'Bacon-wrapped bullet' is a pun (the nearby pork-processing farm, and a bullet dodged) that's so groan-worthy it loops back to being delightful. Then Roland claims to have said 'life is but a strand of happy accidents' before — which he hasn't.
jokes.explanation: 'Bacon-wrapped bullet' is a strong bit of wordplay specific to the situation. Roland's 'I'll say it again' for something he's never said is a running gag established within the same scene — a micro-callback. His accidental wisdom is signature Roland.

jokes.text: You've never said that before.
jokes.setup: Roland says 'I'll say it again: life is but a strand of happy accidents.'
jokes.punchline: David flatly corrects the record — Roland has never said this.
jokes.explanation: Perfect four-word callback. David's precision in correcting Roland is funny because he was listening to something Roland said, which implies a level of attention to Roland he'd rather not admit. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ted's devastated, obviously, but he did say that it's probably for the best because he still hasn't lizard-proofed his place and I have absolutely no desire to be there until that happens.
jokes.setup: Alexis reports back on Ted's reaction to her delayed arrival.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Ted's house needs to be 'lizard-proofed' — implying there are currently free-roaming lizards — and that Alexis has a firm policy on this is absurdly specific and funny.
jokes.explanation: The 'lizard-proofed' detail is excellent — specific to Ted's job (he works with reptiles/wildlife in the Galapagos context) and funny because it's delivered as a dealbreaker with complete Alexis conviction. 'I have absolutely no desire to be there until that happens' is perfect character voice.

jokes.text: You see? Further proof that life is but a... — Okay. We get it. — Strand.
jokes.setup: Roland tries to deploy his new philosophy again.
jokes.punchline: David cuts him off before he finishes. Roland completes it anyway: 'Strand.' The single word completion is funnier than repeating the whole phrase.
jokes.explanation: The running gag reaches its peak here. David cutting Roland off and Roland completing it with just 'Strand.' is perfect — the joke is that Roland gets the last word anyway. The structure of interruption + single-word completion is elegantly economical. Earns strong scores for the structure and the escalation of the callback.

jokes.text: Hey, what if we got married here? — What if we got married under a highway overpass?
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests getting married at the Rosebud Motel instead of finding another venue.
jokes.punchline: David's instant counter-suggestion — 'a highway overpass' — is an absurdist equivalence that reveals how strongly he resists the idea.
jokes.explanation: David's escalation to a highway overpass as the equivalent alternative to the motel is sharp and character-specific. The specificity of 'highway overpass' — not just 'parking lot' or 'anywhere else' — makes it funnier. Good economy.

jokes.text: Oh, in other news: your father was arrested for indecent exposure.
jokes.setup: The family moment is wrapping up warmly.
jokes.punchline: Moira drops Johnny's arrest completely casually as a postscript to the episode — 'in other news' — as though it's a minor footnote.
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode button. The 'in other news' construction — used for trivial matters — applied to 'your father was arrested for indecent exposure' is masterful. It's the culmination of the creek storyline, delivered with maximum Moira nonchalance at the worst possible moment (David and Alexis are reconciling, everyone is eating). The timing and the casualness earn the 8+.

jokes.text: Okay. There's no backing out now!
jokes.setup: The motel/venue decision has been made but everyone is distracted by Johnny's arrest revelation.
jokes.punchline: Alexis cheerfully declares 'no backing out now' — redirecting to the wedding decision as though the indecent exposure announcement didn't just happen.
jokes.explanation: A functional episode-closing beat. Alexis's oblivious cheerfulness is character-true. Not especially sharp writing but a warm, functional button.

tmdb_overview: David scouts a potential wedding venue, while Johnny and Moira have a romantic picnic by the creek.
insight: Season finale opener leans character comedy over punchlines, establishing emotional stakes.
